name: Nuaym ibn Abdullah
title: Nuaym ibn Abdullah (RA): The Man Who Redirected History
meta_description: The story of Nuaym ibn Abdullah (RA), the quiet early Muslim of Banu Adi who sent Umar home with a few wise words and changed the course of that day. A life of hidden faith, service, and care for the forgotten.
keywords: Nuaym ibn Abdullah, an-Nahham, story of Nuaym, early Muslims, companions of the Prophet, Banu Adi, Umar ibn al-Khattab, sahaba stories
lede: One morning in Makkah, a man walked through the streets with his sword drawn, on his way to kill the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Another man saw him, read the danger in an instant, and said one sentence that turned him around.

This is the story of Nuaym ibn Abdullah (may Allah be pleased with him): one of the earliest believers, a man so loved by his people that they begged him to stay, and the quiet companion whose single moment of wisdom helped redirect the course of a day that history never forgot.

sections.h2: A name you may not know
paras: Some companions are famous for a single moment. Nuaym ibn Abdullah is one of them. The moment is well known. His name is not.

He was from Banu Adi, the same clan as Umar ibn al-Khattab, which made the two of them cousins. He accepted Islam very early. In one narration only ten people had embraced the faith before him, which places him among the first eleven Muslims on earth.

He was young when he believed, and he was beloved by his people long before that. They knew him as a pleasant man, a man of fine character, and above all a man of generosity. His generosity was not the kind that comes from having a great deal of wealth. It was the generosity of someone always willing to help, always ready to serve, always looking to take care of others. If you needed him, he was there. No one had a complaint against him.

It is a quiet kind of fame, the kind that does not get written into the great accounts but is felt every day by the people around you. He was praised for his character and for his open hand, and that reputation would matter later, when his clan had to decide what to do with a man who had joined a faith they did not share.

Although Nuaym believed early, he kept his Islam hidden among his people for a long time. He was not hiding out of fear of a known persecution aimed at him. He simply did not make his faith public yet, and he carried it close while the danger to the believers grew around him.

This is worth pausing on. We often picture the first Muslims as a small band who declared themselves and were hunted for it. Nuaym reminds us that some of them held their faith inwardly for years, known to Allah before they were known to anyone else. His belief was real, his place among the first was secure, and yet from the outside he still looked like one of his own people.

There is a quiet dignity in that. He did not need recognition to be a believer. His faith was not weakened by being unseen, and his role among his people, the trust they placed in him, was not yet disturbed by it.
It was in those quiet years that the most famous moment of his life took place, on a day when his cousin Umar set out with murder in mind.

sections.h2: The morning Umar drew his sword
paras: Nuaym was in the streets of Makkah when he saw Umar ibn al-Khattab marching with his sword unsheathed, his face unable to hide his fury. Nuaym asked him where he was going.

Umar told him plainly: he was on his way to Muhammad ibn Abdullah ﷺ, to end what he saw as the tribulation tearing the people of Makkah apart. He did not know that Nuaym, the cousin he was speaking to, was already a Muslim.

Nuaym did not argue and did not panic. He answered with a quiet redirection. You should look to your own household first, he told him, because they too have entered this religion. It was that detour, that single sentence, that sent Umar home instead of to the Prophet ﷺ. There Umar found his own sister, and there his heart was turned, and he went on to the Prophet ﷺ not to harm him but to accept Islam. That was a day of strength for the Muslims, and Nuaym's calm words had helped set it in motion.

Think about how small that act looks from the outside. A man stops his cousin in the street and points him toward home. There is no sermon in it, no confrontation, no risk taken loudly. Yet it changed where Umar walked that day, and that turn made all the difference. Nuaym did not stand in front of a drawn sword and preach. He simply gave it somewhere else to go.

sections.h2: The man they would not let leave
paras: Some time after Umar, Nuaym openly declared his Islam. Yet when the migration to Madinah came, something unusual happened. His own clan, Banu Adi, asked him not to leave.

They knew by now that he was a Muslim, but they did not persecute him and they did not drive him out. The reason was simple. Nuaym was responsible for so many widows and orphans among his people that they could not imagine the community without him. Stay on the religion you have chosen, they told him. We will not harass you and we will not run you out. Only stay, because so many here depend on you.

So he stayed. He did not migrate to Abyssinia and he did not migrate to Madinah, not because his faith was weak, but because of the good he was holding up. The people who relied on him needed him in Makkah, and he carried that weight rather than set it down.

sections.h2: Forty hearts, and a kiss on the head
paras: The day Nuaym finally came to the Prophet ﷺ was at Hudaybiyyah, as the Muslims made their way toward Makkah from Madinah. He did not come alone. He brought with him forty new Muslims from Banu Adi, the very clan that had once harmed others for their faith. Over the years he had won them all to Islam.

While the Prophet ﷺ was receiving the pledge of the companions from Madinah, he was also receiving the shahada of forty people from Makkah arriving with Nuaym. When he saw Nuaym, he stood, embraced him, and kissed him on the head. Your people are better than my people, he said, your home is better than my home.

Nuaym, in his humility, answered that it was the Prophet's people who were better. But the Prophet ﷺ explained his meaning gently. My people drove me out, he said, and your people held you back and supported you. Banu Adi had been patient with Nuaym, and in time he had brought them to the greatest good of all.

Nuaym then offered his own tender reply. Your people expelled you, he said, but Allah gave you the reward of the migration in return. My people held me back from that migration. He was telling the Prophet ﷺ that even the harm done to him had been turned by Allah into reward and blessing.

From that day, Nuaym was with the Prophet ﷺ. He lived on to serve as a soldier through the years that followed. He gave his allegiance to Abu Bakr as caliph, and then to Umar, the same cousin he had once turned away from the door of the Prophet ﷺ.

Picture his life through his own eyes. He had watched Umar storm the streets of Makkah with a sword meant for the Prophet ﷺ. Now he stood among the believers, giving his pledge to that same Umar as leader of the Muslims, and marching out under his command.

He died as a martyr at the Battle of Yarmouk, during the rule of Umar. A man who had begun by quietly redirecting one furious heart ended his life giving everything for the faith he had carried so long in silence.

Allah is the best of planners. The detour Nuaym suggested in a single moment, almost in passing, became part of a turning point that the people of Makkah never forgot. He could not have known what that one sentence would set in motion. He only said the wise thing in front of him, and left the rest to Allah.
There is one more thing about Nuaym, and it is the part that lingers most. He was known by a nickname: an-Nahham. It came from a remarkable saying of the Prophet ﷺ.

On the night of the ascension, when the Prophet ﷺ was taken up, he said that he entered Paradise and heard the nahma of Nuaym. A nahma, as most scholars explain, is the small sound a person makes when they clear their throat. So the Prophet ﷺ was saying that while he was in Paradise, he heard Nuaym there, simply clearing his throat.

From that his people called him an-Nahham. It was a quiet glad tiding for a quiet man. The same Prophet ﷺ who heard the footsteps of Bilal in Paradise also heard the throat of Nuaym, may Allah be pleased with him. A man who played what looked like a small role was promised a place in the gardens, heard there before he had even arrived.

lessons_intro: His story is not built on a battlefield or a famous speech. It is built on a single wise sentence, years of quiet service, and a faith held close. That is exactly why it speaks to ordinary lives.

lessons.bold: A few calm words can change a great deal.
lessons.text: Nuaym did not lecture his furious cousin. He read the moment and said one thing that turned him home. Wisdom is often a matter of the right words at the right moment, not the loudest ones.

lessons.bold: Faith does not always need an audience.
lessons.text: He carried his Islam quietly for years, known to Allah long before he was known to people. What we do unseen still counts, and still matters.

lessons.bold: Caring for the forgotten is its own station.
lessons.text: His clan would not let him leave because so many widows and orphans leaned on him. Looking after the people no one else counts is a kind of greatness that rarely makes the headlines.

lessons.bold: Steadiness over a lifetime is its own reward.
lessons.text: He served quietly, then gave his pledge, then died a martyr. A life of patient service can end in the highest places without ever seeking attention.

faq.a: He was one of the earliest companions of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, from the clan of Banu Adi and a cousin of Umar ibn al-Khattab. In one narration he was among the first eleven Muslims. He is remembered for the day he redirected Umar with a few wise words and for his long service and care for the poor.

The Prophet ﷺ said that on the night of the ascension he entered Paradise and heard the nahma of Nuaym, the small sound of him clearing his throat. From that his people gave him the nickname an-Nahham, a quiet glad tiding of a home in Paradise.

faq.q: Why did Nuaym not migrate to Madinah?
faq.a: His own clan, Banu Adi, asked him to stay because he was responsible for so many widows and orphans among them. They promised not to persecute him. He remained in Makkah for the sake of the people who depended on him, and later came to the Prophet ﷺ at Hudaybiyyah with forty new Muslims.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Nuaym?
faq.a: That a few wise words can change the course of a day, that faith held quietly still counts with Allah, that caring for the forgotten is a kind of greatness, and that a lifetime of steady service can end in the highest reward.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is tempting to think that the people who matter most are the ones at the center of the story. Nuaym was almost never at the center. He stood at the edge, said one calm sentence, looked after the widows no one else counted, and carried his faith without announcing it. And yet a turning point in Islamic history passed through his hands.

Most of our days are like his. We are not on a stage. We are in the street, in the home, beside someone whose heart could be steadied or hardened by what we say next. His life asks us to be that quiet, wise presence: to choose our words with care, to serve the people who depend on us, and to keep our faith sincere even when no one is watching. Carry one of those into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Nuaym ibn Abdullah (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

Another man saw him, and said one sentence that turned him around.

His name was Nuaym ibn Abdullah.

From the clan of Banu Adi, a cousin of Umar, and among the first eleven people on earth to accept Islam.

He carried his faith quietly for years.

Known to Allah long before he was known to people.

Then he saw Umar marching with his sword, furious, on his way to the Prophet ﷺ.

Nuaym did not argue. He said: look to your own household first. They too have entered this religion.

That one sentence sent Umar home instead.

There his heart was turned, and he came to the Prophet ﷺ not to harm him, but to believe.

When the migration came, his own clan begged him to stay.

He cared for so many widows and orphans that they could not imagine the community without him.

He came to the Prophet ﷺ at last with forty new Muslims he had won.

The Prophet ﷺ stood, embraced him, and kissed him on the head.

The Prophet ﷺ said he entered Paradise and heard Nuaym clearing his throat there.

A quiet glad tiding, for a quiet man.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
One morning in Makkah, Umar ibn al-Khattab walked the streets with his sword drawn, on his way to kill the Prophet ﷺ. His own cousin, Nuaym ibn Abdullah (may Allah be pleased with him), saw him and said one calm sentence: look to your own household first.

That single redirection sent Umar home, where his heart was turned to faith. Nuaym was among the first eleven Muslims, yet he carried his Islam quietly for years and stayed in Makkah to care for the widows and orphans who depended on him. He later came to the Prophet ﷺ with forty new believers, and died a martyr at Yarmouk.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The one sentence that turned Umar around
email.alt_subject: He heard him clearing his throat in Paradise
email.preview: A quiet companion, a furious cousin with a sword, and the wise words that helped redirect a day.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

One morning in Makkah, Umar ibn al-Khattab walked through the streets with his sword drawn. He was on his way to kill the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

His own cousin saw him. His name was Nuaym ibn Abdullah (may Allah be pleased with him), and few people today know it, though almost everyone knows the moment.

Nuaym was among the very first to believe. In one narration, only ten people had accepted Islam before him. He was loved by his people for his fine character and his generosity, the kind that comes not from wealth but from always being there when someone needs you. And for years, he kept his own faith quiet, known to Allah before it was known to anyone else.

So when he saw Umar marching with that sword, he did not argue and he did not panic. He simply said: look to your own household first, because they too have entered this religion.

That one sentence sent Umar home instead of to the Prophet ﷺ. There his heart was turned, and he came to the Prophet not to harm him, but to accept Islam.

There is more to Nuaym than that moment. When the migration came, his own clan asked him to stay, because so many widows and orphans depended on him. They promised not to harass him, and he remained for their sake, carrying the people who leaned on him rather than setting them down. Years later he came to the Prophet ﷺ at Hudaybiyyah with forty new Muslims he had quietly won from that same clan. He served on as a soldier and died a martyr at the Battle of Yarmouk.

The Prophet ﷺ once said that on the night of his ascension he entered Paradise and heard Nuaym there, simply clearing his throat. From that, his people called him an-Nahham: a quiet glad tiding for a quiet man.

His life carries a gentle lesson. We are rarely at the center of the story. But a few wise words, said calmly at the right moment, can change the course of a day.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: One morning in Makkah, a man walked through the streets with his sword drawn, on his way to kill the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Another man saw him, read the danger in an instant, and said one sentence that turned him around.

His name was Nuaym ibn Abdullah (may Allah be pleased with him). He was from the clan of Banu Adi, a cousin of Umar ibn al-Khattab, and among the first eleven people on earth to accept Islam. His people loved him for his character and his generosity, the kind that came from always being there when someone needed him.

For years he kept his faith quiet, known to Allah before he was known to anyone else. So on the day he saw Umar marching with his sword, his face full of fury, Nuaym did not argue. He asked where he was going, and Umar said he was on his way to end what he saw as the tribulation among his people.

Look to your own household first, Nuaym told him, because they too have entered this religion. That single sentence sent Umar home instead. There his heart was turned, and he went on to the Prophet ﷺ not to harm him, but to believe. A day of strength for the Muslims had passed through a few quiet words.

When the migration to Madinah came, Nuaym's own clan asked him not to leave. He was responsible for so many widows and orphans that they could not picture the community without him. So he stayed, carrying that weight, for the sake of the people who leaned on him.

Years later he came to the Prophet ﷺ at Hudaybiyyah, bringing forty new Muslims he had won from his own clan. The Prophet ﷺ stood, embraced him, and kissed him on the head. Nuaym served on as a soldier, gave his pledge to Abu Bakr and then to Umar, and died a martyr at the Battle of Yarmouk.

And there is one more thing. The Prophet ﷺ said that on the night of his ascension he entered Paradise and heard Nuaym there, simply clearing his throat. From that his people called him an-Nahham, a quiet glad tiding for a man who never sought the center of any story.
storybook.pullquote: Look to your own household first. They too have entered this religion.
storybook.reflection: Nuaym was almost never at the center of the story. He stood at the edge, said one calm sentence, cared for the widows no one else counted, and held his faith without announcing it. His life is an invitation to be that quiet, wise presence: to choose our words with care, to serve the people who depend on us, and to keep our faith sincere when no one is watching.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
